figures.gloss: The sky and weather 'old man', ruler of cloud and rain, who strikes thunder with his hammer or axe Ukonvasara and bends the rainbow as his bow — the supreme god the Karelian farmer prayed to for the harvest.
figures.facet: A storm-wielder who is NOT a cognate: Ukko wears the same hammer and thunder as Thor and Perun yet descends from no *Perkwunos and no *Dyeus — a Uralic god who arrived at the sky-father's office by independent road, proof the joint can be reached without the Indo-European word.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed chiefly from the Kalevala (Lönnrot, 1835/1849) and the runo-song archive behind it; the heathen layer is read beneath heavy Christian overlay (Ukko later conflated with the Christian God).

figures.gloss: The virgin daughter of the air who descends to the primal sea and floats upon the waters for ages until a duck nests upon her knee; from the broken eggs and from her own shaping hand the world is made, and she becomes the Water-Mother who bears Väinämöinen.
figures.facet: Creation without a creator-king: the cosmos is not commanded into being but slowly shaped by a floating mother's knee and hand in the deep — a maternal, oceanic genesis where the Indo-European pantheons set a sky-lord or a sacrificial body.

figures.gloss: The diving duck lays its eggs on Ilmatar's knee; when they roll into the sea and shatter, the lower shell becomes the earth, the upper the dome of sky, the yolk the sun, the white the moon, the speckles the stars — the whole world hatched from a broken egg in the water.
figures.facet: World-egg and earth-diver fused in one image: the egg supplies the cosmic membrane the world hatches from, while its sinking into the primal sea and rising as land carries the diving-creation pattern — two of the great cosmogonic joints folded into a single shattering.

figures.gloss: The primordial sage born aged from the Water-Mother, the eternal singer whose songs shape boats, sink foes into bogs, and sustain the people; he gives Kaleva's land its first sowing, its instruments and its laws, then departs over the sea promising to return when needed.
figures.facet: The hero whose weapon is the WORD: where other culture-heroes steal fire or forge tools, Väinämöinen makes and unmakes the world by sung incantation — knowledge as magic, the rune-singer as demiurge, a shard where the spell IS the civilising act.
figures.provenance: A composite of older Karelian runo-figures synthesised by Lönnrot into a single epic protagonist; the departing-savior ending is an old motif Christianised into a Christ-yielding farewell.

figures.gloss: The eternal smith, 'the one who hammered out the air', who forged the very dome of the sky so finely that no hammer-mark shows, and afterward forged the Sampo, the mill of endless plenty.
figures.facet: The smith who forged the SKY itself: most divine smiths make weapons, ornaments or the gods' halls, but Ilmarinen's first work is the firmament — the craftsman raised from armourer to cosmic architect, the heavens his earliest forging.

figures.gloss: The mysterious mill forged by Ilmarinen that grinds out grain, salt and gold without end — a talisman of inexhaustible plenty; contested for, broken in the struggle, and its fragments wash ashore to seed Finland's prosperity.
figures.facet: Abundance you can lose: the Sampo is no eternal cornucopia but a made thing fought over and shattered, its scattered shards the source of all later wealth — prosperity figured as a fragile artifact whose very breaking distributes the world's goods.
figures.provenance: The Sampo's exact nature is genuinely unsettled in scholarship (world-pillar, mill, idol, sky-dome?); the Kalevala leaves it deliberately numinous.

figures.gloss: The gap-toothed mistress of Pohjola, the cold dark North, a powerful shamanic sorceress and matriarch who guards the Sampo, sets the heroes their impossible tasks, and at the climax steals the sun and moon and hides them in a hill of stone.
figures.facet: The adversary as a sovereign mother, not a monster: Louhi is no chaos-dragon but a rival ruler with her own land, daughters and rights, who darkens the world by theft of the lights — opposition figured as a competing household rather than as evil incarnate.

figures.gloss: The land of the dead beyond a dark river, ruled by Tuoni and his wife Tuonetar; their daughter ferries the living across, and Väinämöinen who journeys there must trick his way out, for none return by the road they came.
figures.facet: An underworld entered by cunning, not conquered: the Finnish land of the dead is a river-girt house one must out-sing and escape, its ferrywoman a daughter of Death — a sober, riverine afterworld with no judgement and no reward, only the cold north water.

figures.gloss: The reckless, amorous warrior-shaman who rides to Tuonela's river and is shot down, hewn apart and cast into the death-stream; his mother rakes the pieces from the black water and sings him back to life with charms and the ointments of the bees.
figures.facet: Resurrection by a mother's song, not a god's mercy: Lemminkäinen is restored not through sacrifice or divine grace but by the rune-magic and grief of his mother re-membering his scattered body — the dying-and-rising joint reached through shamanic re-assembly.

figures.gloss: The lord and lady of the forest, the bearded green king Tapio and his consort Mielikki, mistress of the woods, who hold the keys to the game and must be praised and coaxed before the hunter's bow finds its quarry.
figures.facet: Divinity as a household of the wilderness: the forest is not a place but a family with whom the hunter must bargain — a sacred ecology where every wood, water and quarry has its keeper to be addressed by name.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An engineered, strictly guarded mechanical object that guarantees the power, survival, and prosperity of the community.

Both are the founding culture-hero inseparable from song: Väinämöinen sings the world into order and Aeoina is the very subject of the oina chanted on the hearth — civilisation and its sung telling fused in one figure. Parallel, no contact.

A loose parallel between two great oral-epic traditions of the North — the chanted yukar and the runo-song world of the Kalevala that Väinämöinen embodies; both keep the gods alive in performed voice without script. Flagged as a reach between a genre and a figure.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme, legendary artifacts forged through intense supernatural labor that generate limitless wealth, health, and cosmic order.
GOLD SEAM: The heavily guarded, heated vessel acting as a microcosm where chaotic elements are incubated until a perfected reality hatches.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A foundational sage who shapes the spiritual and cultural landscape of an entire people through sacred song and esoteric wisdom.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Sovereigns of the forest who control the release of game to hunters and must be ritually propitiated.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial shaman-hero who uses esoteric knowledge to stabilize the world and protect his people.

Cosmos hatched from a primordial egg — the Bön srid-pa egg and the Kalevala's egg of creation as the world-egg joint reached independently; the Bön telling diverges by splitting the egg into light and dark moieties.

The world held in order by sung tale and potent word — the drung-and-deu priest and the Kalevala's singer-sage both work reality through narrative and coded utterance; analogous word-power, separate families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an ultimate, magical source of endless material prosperity and abundance that is fought over and coveted.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A tangible, created artifact that serves as an engine of healing, abundance, and cosmic realignment for the people.
GOLD SEAM: Primordial sowers and culture-heroes who ensure that humanity has both the physical seed and the spiritual knowledge needed to thrive.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A northern sky and weather deity overseeing the forest cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A dark, mythological river dividing the world of the living from the land of the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The personified master of the boreal forest who controls the availability of game.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate, mythological artifact of wealth and metaphysical power that ensures the prosperity and cohesion of the people.

Within the Kalevala the sky-god Ukko and the sky-smith Ilmarinen share the dome of heaven — Ukko rules the weather above the firmament that Ilmarinen hammered; the names may even share an air/sky root within Finnic.
Ilmatar the Water-Mother bears Väinämöinen from the sea after the world is made — mother and first-born hero of the same cosmogony, attested as one continuous opening of the epic.
The egg is laid upon Ilmatar's own knee and shaped by her hand into the world — the Water-Mother and the cosmic egg are two halves of a single creation scene.
Sage-singer and sky-smith are the twinned makers of Kaleva — Väinämöinen commissions and Ilmarinen forges (the sky, the Sampo); the epic's central working partnership.
Ilmarinen forges the Sampo as his masterwork after forging the sky — the smith and his greatest artifact, the act that drives the whole second half of the epic.
Louhi receives the Sampo as bride-price and guards it in Pohjola's stone hill; the heroes' raid to reclaim it and its breaking is the epic's climax.
Louhi of the dark North against Väinämöinen of the sunlit Kaleva — rival shaman-singers and households whose contest over the Sampo and the stolen lights is the spine of the Kalevala.
Lemminkäinen is slain at the river of Tuonela and dismembered into its black water — his death and resurrection are bound to the geography of the Finnish underworld.
Väinämöinen journeys into Tuonela to win the words of a charm and must out-trick Death to return — the hero's descent and escape, a fixed episode of the runo cycle.
Hammer-and-thunder sky-gods who smite with a striking-tool — but Ukko is Uralic and Thor Indo-European (*þunraz); the shared weapon is convergence or areal influence, emphatically NOT a cognate.

Both supreme storm-fathers of the northern forests wielding axe and thunderbolt; Slavic contact may have coloured the Finnic image, but Perun's *Perkwunos name has no Uralic descendant — a borrowed motif at most, not shared blood.

Neighbouring storm-gods of the Baltic shore, Ukko and Perkūnas share thunder, axe and the rainbow-bow through long Balto-Finnic contact — a real areal rhyme across the IE/Uralic divide, never a common name.
Both the chief sky-father wielding the bolt, but from utterly separate families — Ukko shows the sky-father office filled with no *Dyeus word behind it, the joint reached by another lineage entirely.
Divine smiths of supreme skill; yet where Hephaestus forges arms and divine ornament, Ilmarinen first forges the dome of heaven itself — parallel craftsmen, the Finn raised to cosmic architect.
Both are cosmic artificers who shape the very vessels and frame of the world; an honest structural parallel across Uralic and Indo-European with no descent between them.
Craftsman-gods whose making is cosmogonic — Ptah thinks and speaks the world, Ilmarinen hammers out its sky; a loose reach across wholly unrelated traditions, flagged as speculative.
Primal mother-figures of a water-and-earth genesis; Ilmatar floats on the deep to shape the land as Papatūānuku is the earth-mother — independent maternal cosmogonies, no contact.
Both are the generative mother from whom the world's order issues; Ilmatar shapes earth and sky from the egg as Gaia brings them forth — parallel great-mothers of creation.
The cosmic egg whose halves become heaven and earth echoes the Hiraṇyagarbha / world-egg of Indic thought — one of humanity's most widely scattered cosmogonic images, here with no shared line.

Both render the first division of the cosmos from an undifferentiated whole; the Finnish egg-halves and the Nahua dual-source are far-flung, unrelated answers to the same question — a distant rhyme, flagged.
Culture-bringers who endow humankind with the arts of living; Väinämöinen gives song, the kantele, sowing and law where Prometheus gives fire — the benefactor-hero on parallel roads.
Both are primordial wonder-working culture-heroes who reshape the world by cunning and craft — Väinämöinen by song, Māui by trickery and the magic hook; structural kin, no contact.

The Kalevala closes with Väinämöinen departing by sea before a virgin-born child, vowing to return when needed — a parting shaped by the Christian Christ overlaid on an older returning-savior motif; a syncretic overprint, not a true cognate.
Powerful, fearsome female sovereigns set against the heroes; Louhi the northern sorceress-queen and the Morrigan share menace and command, but the parallel is loose and the families unrelated.
River-bounded lands of the dead one crosses by ferry and from which return is barred; Tuonela parallels Hades' realm and Styx, yet without judgement or reward — a colder, plainer underworld.
Northern underworlds of cold and mist ruled by a death-keeping family; Tuonela and Hel rhyme as bleak realms of the departed across the Uralic/Germanic line, by neighbourhood not descent.
The daughter of Tuoni who ferries souls over the death-river fills the same office as Charon — the psychopomp-boatman convergently invented at opposite ends of Europe.

Both are dismembered and gathered back together by a devoted woman — Lemminkäinen's mother rakes his pieces from the river as Isis collects Osiris; the re-membered, resurrected body, independently arrived at.
The torn-apart god restored to life rhymes with the sparagmos and rebirth of Dionysus, but the Finnish version is shamanic mother-magic, not mystery-cult — a distant structural echo, flagged.
Tutelary keepers of the wild presiding over a sacred natural order; Mielikki of the forest loosely parallels the great mother-of-the-land figures — a soft, divergent rhyme rather than a firm joint.

Some scholars read the Sampo as a world-pillar or sky-dome holding the cosmos, which would set it beside the world-axis like Yggdrasil — but the Sampo's nature is genuinely unsettled, so the seam is flagged speculative.
GOLD SEAM: A self-contained macrocosmic shell from which the initiated consciousness undergoes transformation and is ultimately reborn.

Both fish a wondrous thing up out of the primal sea with hook and line — Māui hauling the islands up, Väinämöinen drawing up the great pike (and the egg-world made on the water) — a loose angling-cosmogony echo, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are wandering, gray-bearded archetypes of esoteric knowledge who wield profound magical power through poetry and song rather than brute force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial singing shaman and culture hero who departs the world but leaves behind magical instruments and healing songs for his people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are foundational, organically rooted culture heroes who teach their people agriculture and survival in a harsh landscape.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ethereal wardens of the wilderness and wild game whose favor must be won before any hunt.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The overarching, often distant sky and weather deity shared across Finno-Ugric cosmological frameworks.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A magical, mobile Finno-Ugric culture hero who directly intervenes on behalf of his people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A magical culture hero who overcomes his brutish adversaries through secret knowledge, cool intellect, and mastery of esoteric arts rather than mere physical force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both operate as the sovereign masters of the deep forest, granting or withholding the bounty of the wild to those who approach with respect.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The high sky and weather god shared broadly across Uralic and Finno-Ugric cosmological frameworks.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Uralic woodland/tundra master who directly controls the hunter's luck and the movement of game.
GOLD SEAM: Primordial shamans and master bards whose music bends the natural world and who harrow the underworld for esoteric secrets.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are ultimate, fabricated engines of supernatural power that generate reality-altering force and wealth for their possessor.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A dark realm of the dead located across a treacherous body of water, where ancestors reside in a shadowy mirror of the living world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Semi-divine sages who shape reality not through brute force, but through acoustic perfection, song, and esoteric knowledge.
GOLD SEAM: Both are enclosed, luminous microcosms incubating the potent seeds of a new cosmic reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A profoundly potent artifact whose concealment or loss plunges the world into a state of lack, waiting to be recovered.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel: the primordial, ancient sage who alone possesses the definitive words and instructions for his people.

Two Uralic thunder-'old-men' of Fennoscandia, the Sámi Horagalles/Tiermes and the Finnish Ukko, sharing hammer, bolt and bow through close Finnic-Sámi neighbourhood — kindred storm-fathers reached without the Indo-European word.

Forest-lords of neighbouring Uralic peoples who hold the keys to the game and must be coaxed before the bow finds its mark — the Sámi Alder-Man and the Finnish green king Tapio, kindred keepers of the woods across the Finnic-Sámi border.

Bleak northern lands of the dead beneath the world ruled by a death-keeping power; the Sámi Jábmeaimo and the Finnish Tuonela rhyme as sober Uralic afterworlds of shadowed second life, by Finnic-Sámi neighbourhood rather than descent.
GOLD SEAM: Mystical, heaven-sent or master-forged metallic objects that physically guarantee the sovereignty, wealth, and survival of the people.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme shaman-singers whose magical voices command nature itself and who undertake harrowing journeys to the land of the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The capricious, easily offended master of the woods who controls access to the forest's bounty.
GOLD SEAM: The primordial, unbroken vessel from which the heavens, earth, and celestial bodies are forged when it physically shatters.

Mari jumo and Finnish jumala share the Finno-Volgaic god-word *juma — a real lexical cognate; the offices grew apart, and the deeper seam is the asymmetry: Ukko is reconstructed from song while Jumo still eats grove sacrifice.

The holy forest as the place where the god is met — but the kusoto is a consecrated sanctuary with priest and altar-fire while Tapio's wood is the wild itself; kin peoples, different offices of the forest.

Mother-powers of Finnic kin peoples, met at different scales: Ilmatar is the one oceanic mother of the cosmogony, Mlande Ava one of a working system of element-mothers tending the tilled land — the same joint, cosmic versus agrarian register.

Inmar and Ilmarinen both grow from the Uralic sky-word *ilma — a real name-kinship across the family; the offices diverged (high sky-lord versus sky-forging smith), so the seam is the shared root, not a shared cult.

The deep-Uralic argument: two ecstatic wonder-workers of related language families read as echoes of a shared shamanic substrate — suggestive, unprovable across four thousand years of separation, and both reconstructed; flagged speculative.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian religion and mythology of the Finns and Karelians (Baltic-Finnic, Uralic-speaking peoples of Fennoscandia), overlaid by Christianity from roughly the 12th-13th centuries but preserved in an immense body of oral 'runo' song. It is known above all through the 'Kalevala', the national epic that Elias Lonnrot compiled and shaped from that oral poetry in 1835 (expanded 1849).
dossier.cosmos: In the runo cosmogony the world is formed not by a commanding sky-god but from a waterbird's egg shattered on the knee of the air-and-water mother Ilmatar, its shell becoming earth and sky and its yolk the sun — a maternal, oceanic genesis. The sky-god Ukko rules thunder and the harvest, the smith Ilmarinen forged the very dome of heaven, and the land of the dead, Tuonela (Manala), lies across a dark river and is escaped only by cunning, with no scheme of judgment or reward. Louhi, mistress of the cold North (Pohjola), is the great rival power.
dossier.practice: Religious efficacy lay above all in the potent sung word — incantations (loitsut) whose power depended on knowing a thing's mythic origin — wielded by the tietaja ('knower', sage-magician), archetype of Vainamoinen, who makes and unmakes the world by song. Historical practice also included cult of the dead, bear ceremonialism (the karhunpeijaiset feast), and the propitiation of the forest-lord Tapio and other nature-guardians before the hunt.
dossier.canon: A vast oral corpus of runo-songs in kalevalaic meter, incantations, and laments, collected in the 19th century and synthesized by Lonnrot into the 'Kalevala' (1835; expanded 1849) and, for the lyric material, the 'Kanteletar' — the epic being a scholarly compilation, not an ancient scripture.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the magic of the named word — a worldview in which knowing the true origin-story of a thing grants verbal power over it, and the poet-magician is the effective religious specialist.
sources: Anna-Leena Siikala, 'Mythic Images and Shamanism: A Perspective on Kalevala Poetry' (2002)
